originalTitle: Congress passes war powers measure for first time, breaking with Trump over Iran
summary: Experts say Trump is likely to ignore it, but that even though it carries little power, it is politically significant.
content:
The Republican-controlled US Senate has approved a measure demanding that President Donald Trump halt the war in Iran or seek congressional approval before continuing military action.

But while the resolution is largely symbolic, it could have political ramifications and represents a rare rebuke of Trump by lawmakers who have largely fallen behind the president since his return to office.

A handful of Republicans joined Democrats in Tuesday's 50-48 vote. The same measure was passed by the US House of Representatives earlier this month.

It came as Republicans in Congress have expressed scepticism about a peace plan Trump agreed with Iran, and the unpopular conflict approaches its fifth month.

Trump criticised the resolution on Tuesday night, calling it "poorly timed and meaningless".

"So, I have Iran on the 'ropes,' ready to go down for the fall... and the U.S. Senate decides to have a poorly timed and meaningless War Powers Act Vote," Trump wrote on his Truth Social platform.

"These Senators have just made my job more difficult, but I will get it done, one way or the other, because I always get it done!"

UN says it will evacuate sailors stranded in Strait of Hormuz, as Rubio warns against tolls

The vote marks the first time that both chambers of Congress have approved a concurrent resolution instructing a president to end a military action since the War Powers Resolution of 1973 was enacted.

A concurrent resolution expresses the sentiment or will of Congress, as opposed to other forms of legislation that go to the president to be signed into law. In 2019, Trump vetoed a joint resolution that called for the removal of armed forces from hostilities in the Yemeni civil war.

Middle East analyst Laura Blumenfeld called it "more of a slap on a wrist than a handcuff, because it has no legal binding".

But she told the BBC she thought what "it does reflect is the American people's sentiments".

In practice, the vote is unlikely to have little impact on the currently frozen conflict with Iran.

Experts believe he is likely to ignore it - as has every US president since it was enacted - and any legal challenges would likely produce no result.

"As a practical matter, it probably doesn't make much difference," said Michael Glennon, a professional law professor at Tufts University in Massachusetts. "It's far more significant politically."

"You have both houses of Congress, which are under the control of the Republican Party, standing up to the president and saying no," Glennon added. "That's almost unprecedented."

Trump, Glennon noted, has been "very effective" in exacting retribution against dissident Republicans who oppose him.

"This is a watershed event, politically, for that reason," he said.

Whether the vote represents a harbinger of more opposition to the president remains unclear, however.

"I wouldn't overreact to the significance of this vote," said Jonathan Entin, a constitutional law professor at Case Western Reserve University in Ohio. "It's important... but I'm not sure that we should view it as a sign of consistent conflict, or additional conflict, with the president."

According to Entin, the upcoming November midterm elections in the US mean that while a handful of Republicans may wish to signal independence from Trump to bring in independent voters, many others will be hoping to avoid drawing his ire.

"There are constraints," he said. "The president has overwhelming support among Republicans. A candidate who pushes back too much might risk alienating strong Republican voters who think they aren't committed enough."

The same measure was passed earlier this month by the US House of Representatives, where four Republicans joined every Democrat to approve it in a 215-208 vote.

But a White House official told the BBC that with the ceasefire agreed on 7 April, there are no hostilities from which to withdraw American forces.

The official also said the measure only passed because two Republican senators were absent: Mitch McConnell and Dave McCormick.

Four Republican senators voted with Democrats in support of the resolution: Rand Paul, Lisa Murkowski, Susan Collins and Bill Cassidy.

Democratic Senator John Fetterman was the only member of his party to vote against it.

It was the latest sign of division among Trump's fellow Republicans ahead of the midterm elections in November, which will determine whether the party can hold on to its slender majorities in both chambers of Congress.

Some Republicans have resisted the president recently, including by rejecting his plans to create a $1.8 bn (£1.3bn) "anti-weaponisation" fund and approving Ukraine aid .

Tuesday's vote was the 10th time that Senate Democrats have forced a war powers vote since the start of the war.

It came the same day as the Pentagon asked Congress for some $80bn, most of which is to pay for the war with Iran.

Federal law requires congressional approval to continue military actions for more than 60 days. The US-Israel strikes on Iran began on 28 February, although the Trump administration has argued April's ceasefire reset the clock.

The White House can also extend the deadline for another 30 days, citing national security.

Currently, the US and Iran have agreed to continue a ceasefire and are working towards an end of hostilities under a memorandum of understanding that was signed by the presidents of both countries last week.

Under that memo, Washington and Tehran have 60 days to negotiate a broader agreement on ending Iran's nuclear programme.

Dozens of ships head through Strait of Hormuz after US-Iran deal

Iran says no new commitments on nuclear sites after Vance says inspectors to be invited back

US House delivers rebuke to Trump as it votes to halt Iran war
chineseTitle: 国会首次通过战争权力措施，在伊朗问题上与特朗普决裂
chineseSummary: 专家表示，特朗普可能会忽视它，但尽管它权力不大，但具有政治意义。
chineseContent:
共和党控制的美国参议院批准了一项措施，要求唐纳德·特朗普总统停止对伊朗的战争或在继续采取军事行动之前寻求国会批准。

不过，尽管该决议在很大程度上是象征性的，但它可能会产生政治影响，并且代表着议员们对特朗普的罕见谴责，自特朗普重返办公室以来，议员们在很大程度上已经落后于特朗普。

在周二的投票中，少数共和党人以 50 比 48 的投票结果加入了民主党的行列。本月早些时候，美国众议院也通过了同样的措施。

国会共和党人对特朗普与伊朗达成的和平计划表示怀疑，这场不受欢迎的冲突已进入第五个月。

特朗普周二晚间批评了该决议，称其“时机不当且毫无意义”。

特朗普在他的真相社交平台上写道：“因此，我让伊朗处于‘绳索’上，准备在秋季倒下……而美国参议院决定进行一次不合时宜且毫无意义的战争权力法案投票。”

“这些参议员让我的工作变得更加困难，但我会以某种方式完成它，因为我总是能完成它！”

卢比奥警告不要过路费 联合国表示将疏散滞留在霍尔木兹海峡的水手

此次投票标志着自1973年《战争权力决议》颁布以来，国会参众两院首次同时批准一项指示总统结束军事行动的决议。

同时提出的决议表达了国会的观点或意愿，这与其他形式的立法不同，这些立法需提交总统签署成为法律。 2019年，特朗普否决了一项呼吁将武装部队撤出也门内战敌对行动的联合决议。

中东分析师劳拉·布卢门菲尔德称其“与其说是手铐，不如说是手腕上的一记耳光，因为它没有法律约束力”。

但她告诉英国广播公司，她认为“这确实反映了美国人民的情绪”。

实际上，这次投票不太可能对目前冻结的与伊朗的冲突产生什么影响。

专家认为，他很可能会忽视该法案——就像该法案颁布以来的每一位美国总统一样——任何法律挑战都可能不会产生任何结果。

马萨诸塞州塔夫茨大学的专业法学教授迈克尔·格伦农说：“实际上，这可能没有多大区别。” “这在政治上意义重大得多。”

格伦农补充道：“共和党控制下的国会参众两院都站起来对总统说不。” “这几乎是前所未有的。”

格伦农指出，特朗普在对反对他的持不同政见的共和党人进行报复方面“非常有效”。

“因此，从政治角度来说，这是一个分水岭事件，”他说。

然而，此次投票是否预示着将有更多人反对总统，目前尚不清楚。

“我不会对这次投票的重要性反应过度，”俄亥俄州凯斯西储大学宪法学教授乔纳森·恩廷说。 “这很重要……但我不确定我们是否应该将其视为与总统持续冲突或额外冲突的迹象。”

恩廷表示，即将到来的美国 11 月中期选举意味着，尽管少数共和党人可能希望发出独立于特朗普的信号以吸引独立选民，但许多其他人希望避免引起他的愤怒。

“存在一些限制，”他说。 “总统在共和党人中拥有压倒性的支持。一位过于反对的候选人可能会冒疏远那些认为自己不够忠诚的强大共和党选民的风险。”

本月早些时候，美国众议院也通过了同样的措施，四名共和党议员与每位民主党议员一起以 215 票对 208 票通过了该法案。

但一名白宫官员告诉 BBC，随着 4 月 7 日达成停火协议，美军不再需要从敌对行动中撤军。

该官员还表示，该措施之所以获得通过，是因为两名共和党参议员缺席：米奇·麦康奈尔和戴夫·麦考密克。

四名共和党参议员与民主党一起投票支持该决议：兰德·保罗、丽莎·默科斯基、苏珊·柯林斯和比尔·卡西迪。

民主党参议员约翰·费特曼是该党内唯一投反对票的成员。

这是特朗普的共和党同僚在11月中期选举之前出现分歧的最新迹象，中期选举将决定该党能否保住国会参众两院的微弱多数席位。

一些共和党人最近对总统提出了抵制，包括拒绝他设立 18 亿美元（13 亿英镑）“反武器化”基金的计划以及批准对乌克兰的援助。

周二的投票是自战争开始以来参议院民主党第十次强制进行战争权力投票。

就在同一天，五角大楼向国会请求提供约 800 亿美元，其中大部分用于支付与伊朗的战争费用。

联邦法律要求国会批准军事行动持续 60 天以上。美国和以色列对伊朗的打击于 2 月 28 日开始，尽管特朗普政府辩称 4 月的停火重新设定了时间。

白宫还可以以国家安全为由将最后期限再延长 30 天。

目前，美国和伊朗已同意继续停火，并正在根据两国总统上周签署的谅解备忘录努力结束敌对行动。

根据该备忘录，华盛顿和德黑兰有 60 天的时间就结束伊朗核计划谈判达成更广泛的协议。

美伊达成协议后数十艘船只驶过霍尔木兹海峡

万斯表示将邀请检查员回来后，伊朗表示没有对核设施做出新的承诺

美国众议院投票停止伊朗战争，谴责特朗普
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于全球新闻信号，适合用于观察外部环境、政策变化、地缘风险和市场情绪。
